evidence: Protocol contract definitions are isolated in `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go` as explicit constants and catalog entries, and StarTram error contracts are isolated in `goseg/protocol/contracts/startram_contracts.go`, preserving package boundary clarity.

The governance section in `README.md` explicitly names the contract surfaces (`goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts.go`, `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go`, `goseg/protocol/contracts/startram_contracts.go`) that own protocol/error contract compatibility policy.
README guidance ties contract behavior to typed APIs (`ContractID`, `ActionNamespace`, `ActionToken`, `ActionContractBinding`) instead of raw string lookups, which reduces cross-module coupling risk.
CI gate mention in `README.md` covers runtime + protocol contract files and contract tests, indicating a cross-module control path for contract changes.
cross_module_architecture.issues_preventing_higher_score: A small remaining risk is that the architecture governance is documentation-driven rather than mechanically enforced in these files alone; documenting and validating module boundary checks in CI/schema checks would make the contract boundary harder to regress.

evidence: Multiple subsystems consume a shared mutable `DefaultBroadcastStateRuntime()` singleton instead of injecting explicit runtime state (`goseg/broadcast/state.go`, `goseg/rectify/rectify.go`, `goseg/routines/pack.go`, `goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go`).
`k`ill-switch flow in `main.go` calls `exec.Command("reboot")` directly, bypassing a runtime seam used elsewhere and forcing a concrete side-effect through command execution.
CLI parsing in `parseStartupOptions` mutates package globals (`HttpPort`, `WebsocketPort`, `DevMode`) instead of returning a pure config value (`goseg/main.go`, `goseg/main_test.go`).

The package-level contract governance list in `README.md` explicitly requires contract-regression tests for protocol and StarTram families and cites `protocol_contracts_test.go` and `startram_contracts_test.go`.

The same README section states a dedicated contract gate (`.github/workflows/upload-contract-gate.yml` and `.github/scripts/check-upload-contract.sh`) for changed contract surface files, indicating a defined strategy for deterministic contract test coverage.
The two contract definition files are pure declaration files with no behavioral branches; test value depends on dedicated companion tests rather than local logic-heavy coverage.
test_strategy.issues_preventing_higher_score: Higher score is constrained by inability to verify from this batch alone that the referenced tests continuously assert all contract-policy invariants (for example, backward-compat descriptors and masked-error semantics) as code evolves.

evidence: goseg/broadcast/state.go exposes cloned reads (`GetState`) but writes raw input into `broadcastState` in `UpdateBroadcast`, creating asymmetric ownership around mutable broadcast state.

multiple listed test files use package-level mutable state resets (`resetLeakStateForTest`, `resetUploadSessionsForTest`, `resetParameterGlobalsForTest`, `resetRouterGlobalsForTest`) and direct global mutation, which makes behavior depend on external mutable process state rather than a localized test fixture.

historically flagged responsibility-cohesion issues in `goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint_test.go`, `goseg/leak/handler_test.go`, and `goseg/importer/importer_test.go` are still visible in current test structure.

goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts.go: ContractDescriptorFor ignores registry initialization errors and collapses them into the same false return as a missing contract (`return ContractDescriptor{}, false`).

goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts.go: MustContractDescriptor converts resolved contract lookup/init failures into panics (`panic(fmt.Sprintf(...))`) instead of returning an error for caller handling.
goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts_test.go: tests explicitly expect panic behavior for unknown IDs, indicating this panic path is part of the API surface and not strictly test-only.

findings.summary: Ambient broadcast state singleton is shared across unrelated modules, reducing modularity and test isolation
evidence: `state.go` defines a package-level `defaultBroadcastStateRuntime` and `DefaultBroadcastStateRuntime()` returns that global pointer.
Production and runtime code in multiple packages repeatedly call `DefaultBroadcastStateRuntime()` directly (rectify, shipworkflow, routines), creating implicit coupling to process-wide mutable state.
Tests also read/write this default runtime, indicating shared state can leak across test boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Stop using the global singleton as the primary runtime contract: introduce explicit runtime injection through constructor params for all broadcast consumers, keep `DefaultBroadcastStateRuntime` as a narrow test/bootstrap helper only, and provide a resettable factory for test isolation.

findings.summary: C2C kill-switch reboot path hardcodes process execution and cannot be abstracted/replaced
evidence: `killSwitchWithMode` executes `exec.Command("reboot")` directly instead of using an injected system-control seam.
Neighboring control flows in this batch consistently pass dependencies via runtime structs (`c2cRuntime`, wifi service wrappers), so this call is an outlier that escapes seam-based design.
findings.suggestion: Add a restart command/policy callback into the C2C runtime struct and call it from `killSwitchWithMode`; default it to reboot in production and inject a no-op/recording implementation in tests/CI to avoid side effects.

findings.summary: Startup option parsing mutates package globals, creating hidden mutable dependencies
evidence: `parseStartupOptions` writes parsed values into globals (`DevMode`, `HttpPort`, `WebsocketPort`) rather than returning immutable config.
Those globals are then observed later by startup/bootstrap behavior, so callers cannot safely reuse parsing in alternative contexts without side effects.
findings.suggestion: Make startup option parsing pure: parse into a dedicated options struct only, keep globals read-only defaults, and pass parsed values explicitly through bootstrap/runtime constructors.

findings.summary: Broadcast state writer stores caller-owned references while readers get clones, allowing external mutation of internal state.
evidence: `UpdateBroadcast` assigns `runtime.broadcastState = next` directly (no defensive copy).
`GetState` returns `cloneBroadcastState(runtime.broadcastState)` for caller-safe reads, creating a write-vs-read ownership mismatch.
This pattern is fragile in concurrent flows where the same state object may be mutated after `UpdateBroadcast` by caller code.
findings.suggestion: Clone the incoming state in `UpdateBroadcast` before storing, e.g. `runtime.broadcastState = cloneBroadcastState(next)`, and optionally add regression tests that mutate the caller-owned payload after update to assert runtime state remains unchanged.

findings.summary: Tests are tightly coupled to package-level globals, creating brittle, mixed-responsibility fixtures across modules.
evidence: `accesspoint_test.go` defines multiple global-state restoration helpers (`resetParameterGlobalsForTest`, `resetRouterGlobalsForTest`) and mutates globals such as `wlan`, `inet`, `ip`, `password`, and runtime function vars.
`importer_test.go` uses package-wide helpers like `resetUploadSessionsForTest` and direct seam mutation around `uploadSessions` for each test.
`leak/handler_test.go` drives behavior by mutating shared global maps/channels and relies on global resets (`resetLeakStateForTest`).
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit fixture builders/runtime structs for tests and replace direct global mutation with constructor-injected seams (or context-bound test doubles) so each test owns isolated, composable state and setup/teardown is declarative rather than global.

findings.summary: Contract catalog lookup APIs mix silent bool-only failures and panic-on-fail APIs, making initialization and missing-descriptor errors indistinguishable to callers.
evidence: ContractDescriptorFor(name) swallows `defaultRegistrySnapshot()` errors and returns `(zero, false)` when registry initialization fails.
MustContractDescriptor wraps the same underlying condition in `panic` for both init failures and missing IDs.
The test for missing contract expects panic (`TestMustContractDescriptorPanicsForUnknownID`), so both recoverable and non-recoverable paths are exposed as first-class behavior.
findings.suggestion: Unify the contract API contract so lookup failures consistently return explicit errors at boundaries: keep only one fallible API for runtime lookups (`ContractDescriptorForWithError`) and make the boolean API return distinct categories (e.g., `exists`, `err`) or document/test it as intentionally lossy, while changing `Must...` to a very narrowly-used startup helper that is clearly documented and isolated to init-time callers.

findings.summary: Normalize forwarded IP before context checks to prevent false token mismatches
evidence: requestIdentityFromRequest() uses strings.Split(r.Header.Get("X-Forwarded-For"), ",")[0] without strings.TrimSpace on the extracted IP.

When `auth.UploadAuthPolicy().RequireRequestContext` is enabled, `AuthorizeUploadToken` compares this raw IP directly against token metadata, so proxy-formatted header variants can fail auth context checks unexpectedly.
findings.suggestion: In requestIdentityFromRequest, trim whitespace on the selected hop (`strings.TrimSpace(strings.Split(...)[0])`) and optionally validate it with net.SplitHostPort/ParseIP normalization before comparison; add a regression test in `goseg/auth/tokens/tokens_test.go` for spaced X-Forwarded-For variants.
